elements.sent-2411-001: In the world of relational database management, the query optimizer is a critical component that significantly impacts query performance.
elements.sent-2411-002: To address the challenge of optimizing query performance due to the complexity of optimizers -- especially with join operations -- we introduce \systemname.
elements.sent-2411-003: This novel visualization tool provides a window into the often intricate process of query optimization in PostgreSQL, making it more accessible and understandable.
elements.sent-2411-004: PostgreSQL employs two different query optimization strategies: the Dynamic Programming (DP) Optimizer for most scenarios and the Genetic Query Optimizer (GEQO) for more complex queries with numerous joins, both of which are supported in \systemname.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our tool visualizes the optimizer's decision-making process, from evaluating access paths for each relation to determining join orderings, all using data derived from the optimizer's logs.
elements.sent-2411-006: The source code has been made available at https://github.com/snu-jovis.
elements.sent-2411-007: In relational database management systems, the query optimizer holds formidable importance as it is a key determinant of query performance.
elements.sent-2411-008: Because SQL is declarative, no information is given as to how to process queries, thus leaving the optimizer responsible for finding an efficient execution plan.
elements.sent-2411-009: This involves planning access methods for each relation and determining the optimal join sequence of relations.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, the optimizer is known to be a highly complex system due to the NP-hard nature of generating the optimal query plan within the vast search space~\cite{np-hard .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Notably, query optimization is NP-hard with respect to the number of joins.
elements.sent-2411-012: Among operators in relational algebra, join is the most challenging one to process and optimize, yet the critical factor of query performance.
elements.sent-2411-013: In PostgreSQL, the optimizer examines possible query plans by generating them using data structures called paths.
elements.sent-2411-014: These paths contain the information the optimizer requires and are evaluated using a cost model.
elements.sent-2411-015: PostgreSQL employs two types of optimizers: the Dynamic Programming (DP) Optimizer and the Genetic Query Optimizer (GEQO).
elements.sent-2411-016: These optimizers differ in the algorithm implemented for determining the optimal join sequence.
elements.sent-2411-017: The DP Optimizer, a standard approach, enumerates query plans by modeling the query graph as a hypergraph~\cite{dp .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, when dealing with queries involving a large number of join operations, the DP Optimizer becomes infeasible due to the excessive time and memory required.
elements.sent-2411-019: In such cases, PostgreSQL switches to using the GEQO.
elements.sent-2411-020: GEQO initially generates a set of possible join sequences randomly.
elements.sent-2411-021: Then, it iteratively generates a new sequence by combining two existing ones using a mechanism called edge recombination crossover (ERX) as the default~\cite{pg-doc-geqo .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Because PostgreSQL uses two types of optimizers, and both of them consider a substantial number of query plans, understanding the complex processes involved in choosing the optimal plan and the rationale behind that choice is highly challenging.
elements.sent-2411-023: To address this problem and enhance comprehension of query optimization in PostgreSQL, visualization tools that offer essential summary and detailed information are necessary.
elements.sent-2411-024: Such tools would benefit both learners aiming to grasp the details of query optimization and practitioners seeking to optimize queries manually (\eg using hints).
elements.sent-2411-025: Additionally, the GEQO has remaining implementation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-026: The tasks involve optimizing parameter settings and reducing redundant work, as the GEQO uses a standard optimizer on join selection and cost estimation from scratch in each step.
elements.sent-2411-027: Also, the other recombination mechanisms can be considered for further optimization of GEQO.
elements.sent-2411-028: By leveraging visualization tools, practitioners can explore the challenge the GEQO holds and evaluate their optimizations by observing cost trends and analyzing chosen join sequences and recombination processes.
elements.sent-2411-029: Despite the necessity of visualizing PostgreSQL's query optimization procedure, particularly in determining the optimal ordering of joins, current visualization tools for query optimizers lack the ability to visualize how optimizers function internally.
elements.sent-2411-030: Instead, they mainly focus on analyzing the optimal query plan that has ultimately been chosen~\cite{qe3d or optimizing the resulting query plan~\cite{qo-insight, mocha .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this demonstration, we present \systemname, an interactive visual exploration tool designed to provide user-friendly and effective visualizations of PostgreSQL's optimizer decision-making process.
elements.sent-2411-032: Given an SQL query, \systemname automatically generates visual representations of the planning procedure of access paths for each relation and join orderings by leveraging the logs emitted during query optimization.
elements.sent-2411-033: For standard optimization using dynamic programming, \systemname depicts the procedure as a directed acyclic graph (DAG), whereas for GEQO implemented with a genetic algorithm, it employs a grid heatmap visualization.
elements.sent-2411-034: Besides, it provides the cost of each path, enhancing the understanding of PostgreSQL's cost-based search and the visualization of ERX executed in GEQO to show how plans for joining relations evolve throughout the generation.
elements.sent-2411-035: Moreover, the resulting optimal query plan passed to the executor is represented as a tree, and various interactive features are provided to enable comprehensive exploration.
elements.sent-2411-036: We will demonstrate \systemname using the TPC-H and TPC-DS decision support benchmarks to signify its usefulness and effectiveness in the exploration of PostgreSQL's query optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-037: We will also use custom queries to ensure the \systemname's applicability beyond the standardized benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-038: Visualization tools for query optimizers have been demonstrated actively in the database community.
elements.sent-2411-039: For example, Picasso~\cite{picasso employs various diagrams to depict the behaviors of the optimizer.}
elements.sent-2411-040: QE3D~\cite{qe3d offers diverse views for visualizing distributed query plans, including three-dimensional representation.}
elements.sent-2411-041: DBinsight~\cite{dbinsight presents a tool for visualizing the query processing pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-042: MOCHA~\cite{mocha provides a tool focusing on operator selection impact, allowing users to switch operators and observe corresponding performance changes.}
elements.sent-2411-043: QO-Insight~\cite{qo-insight targets steered query optimizers, offering a visual interface for exploring query execution traces.}
elements.sent-2411-044: While these existing tools share the objective of providing interactive visualization for query optimizers, \systemname distinguishes itself by focusing on the optimizer's procedural aspects.
elements.sent-2411-045: It showcases cost estimation for each query plan at every stage, offering a comprehensive understanding of the optimizer's decision.
elements.sent-2411-046: With a user-friendly interface, \systemname facilitates exploration of these visualizations, helping users gain insights that can potentially be leveraged to optimize query plans or even the optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-047: Efforts have also been made to develop a functional system capable of building, presenting, and browsing visualizations of genetic algorithm data~\cite{cruz .}
elements.sent-2411-048: The visualization approach adopted by \systemname is based on this work.
elements.sent-2411-049: However, \systemname is tailored specifically to GEQO, using the optimizer's unique data extracted from the logs and supporting features essential for exploring genetic query optimization.
elements.sent-2411-050: Notably, to the best of our knowledge, there is currently no visualization tool dedicated to GEQO.
elements.sent-2411-051: This section provides an overview of the architecture of \systemname's illustrated in Figure ~\ref{fig:arc .}
elements.sent-2411-052: It consists of a graphical user interface (GUI) with visualization components for both the DP Optimizer and GEQO and a backend containing the query submitter and parser.
elements.sent-2411-053: The \systemname GUI, illustrated in Figure ~\ref{fig:gui , is implemented using the React library and D3.js~\cite{d3 for data-driven visualizations and user interactions.. A.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: In the SQL editor, users input their queries, select the desired database, and then submit the query for execution.
elements.sent-2411-055: Upon completion of query execution, the optimziation procedure for both the DP Optimizer and GEQO is visualized using distinctive graph types to illustrate the distinct optimization procedures effectively.
elements.sent-2411-056: Besides, \systemname supports subqueries, including those with \code{WITH clauses, by enabling the visualization of multiple optimizer processes concurrently.. B.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In the History tab, \systemname maintains a record of previous queries submitted by users, and in the Presets tab, TPC-H and TPC-DS queries are provided.
elements.sent-2411-058: These features aim to make the testing process more accessible to users.. Dynamic Programming Optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-059: The view C is specifically designed to visualize the DP optimization procedure effectively.
elements.sent-2411-060: The DP Optimizer is the standard optimizer and is invoked when the number of relations to join is below the \code{geqo\_threshold .}
elements.sent-2411-061: This threshold parameter informs PostgreSQL when to utilize either the DP optimizer or the GEQO, providing flexibility in optimization strategies based on query complexity~\cite{pg-doc-geqo .. C1.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The DP Optimizer models the query graph as a hypergraph.
elements.sent-2411-063: It adopts a bottom-up approach, illustrating relations as nodes and operators as edges.
elements.sent-2411-064: Through iterative steps, it incrementally constructs connected sub-graphs by adding new edges to nodes until the complete query plan is achieved.
elements.sent-2411-065: At each iteration, the costs of new operators are estimated.
elements.sent-2411-066: DAG is effective in visualizing the bottom-up strategy since DAG can have multiple parent nodes.
elements.sent-2411-067: To effectively depict this bottom-up strategy, \systemname employs a DAG due to its ability to have multiple children and parents, which fits the characteristics of the optimization process where various access paths are considered.
elements.sent-2411-068: In the DAG, rectangular nodes represent access paths, including the scan or join strategies executable towards their upper node, while circular nodes represent relation or join sequences of multiple relations.
elements.sent-2411-069: Each node is color-coded to distinguish between different types of access paths, enabling easy identification.
elements.sent-2411-070: The use of DAG helps users visually grasp all the access paths considered during the query optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-071: Moreover, \systemname's support for subqueries enables users to inspect multiple DAGs, each corresponding to a specific subquery.
elements.sent-2411-072: By setting the scale as cost, the size of operator nodes adjusts based on their estimated cost.
elements.sent-2411-073: This clearly shows why the optimizer chose a particular operator.
elements.sent-2411-074: Also, initiating the animation by pressing the Play button allows users to observe the chosen access path for each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-075: The opacity of the non-selected node is reduced, enabling users to track both current selections and previously evaluated options.
elements.sent-2411-076: This feature offers dynamic exploration, from selecting the best access path for a single relation to navigating the decision-making process for identifying the cheapest total join cost.. C2.
elements.sent-2411-077: Within the DAG, users can interact with operator nodes by clicking on them to access all the cost metrics, including total cost, run cost, and startup cost.
elements.sent-2411-078: For operator nodes representing joins, \systemname provides a simplified cost calculation formula, helping in understanding the cost calculation process of each join strategy.. C3.
elements.sent-2411-079: The optimal query plan results from query optimization, which can be retrieved using the \code{EXPLAIN command.}
elements.sent-2411-080: When queries contain subqueries, a single DAG cannot cover the entire query plan.
elements.sent-2411-081: Instead, the optimal query plan for the entire query is illustrated using a tree.
elements.sent-2411-082: This design choice is motivated by the inherent tree format of \code{EXPLAIN ANALYZE result, where a root node labeled \code{Plans branches into nested \code{Plan nodes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: To deal with the cases where the tree becomes excessively large due to a complex query plan, zoom and movable feature is implemented to enhance usability.. Genetic Query Optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-084: The view D is designed to visualize the GEQO procedure.
elements.sent-2411-085: GEQO is invoked when the number of join operations hits or exceeds the \code{geqo\_threshold .}
elements.sent-2411-086: Throughout the steps called generations, each generation maintains a pool comprising candidate join sequences, called genes.
elements.sent-2411-087: Genes with lower estimated costs are thought of as more fit.
elements.sent-2411-088: Each generation creates a new gene using two existing genes, and the least fit gene is discarded from the pool.
elements.sent-2411-089: This iterative process continues until a preset number of join sequences have been evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-090: Note that \code{geqo\_threshold is set to 5 in Figure ~\ref{fig:gui for illustration purposes, but it performs effectively with larger values such as the default value of 12.. D1.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Inspired by the genetic algorithm visualization by Cruz~\cite{cruz , \systemname utilizes a grid heatmap to visualize the genes within pools throughout the generations.}
elements.sent-2411-092: The heatmap represents the estimated cost of genes using color gradients, implemented using d3-interpolate.
elements.sent-2411-093: As shown in D1a, by color-coding each square, the convergence of costs across generations becomes apparent, which reveals the effectiveness of GEQO in improving fitness.
elements.sent-2411-094: Furthermore, range sliders have been implemented to help users focus on specific generations and genes they want to explore.
elements.sent-2411-095: As depicted in D1b, when the generation range is narrowed down to a predefined threshold, the visualization of ERX appears for finer insights.
elements.sent-2411-096: The relationship between parents and their offspring is illustrated using edges.. D2.
elements.sent-2411-097: The cost chart is designed as a line chart to present the best, worst, median, and average estimated cost throughout the generations.
elements.sent-2411-098: Users can hover over the line to pinpoint the generation they wish to analyze, and precise values for each metric are shown.
elements.sent-2411-099: A logarithmic scale for the y-axis has been implemented to address issues with visualizing smaller metric trends when there's a significant gap in values between metrics.
elements.sent-2411-100: Users can toggle between linear and logarithmic scales through checkbox; this adds flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-101: Also, users can focus on a specific metric of their interest by clicking it in the legend.. D3.
elements.sent-2411-102: Users can interact with the heatmap by clicking on a specific gene they want to explore.
elements.sent-2411-103: The join sequence of the clicked gene is displayed as a tree, with each operator's cost represented through a stacked bar chart.
elements.sent-2411-104: Within this chart, users can detect the percentage contribution of each operator to the total cost.
elements.sent-2411-105: By clicking on individual stacks, the corresponding operator in the tree is marked as red, and its estimated cost and percentage are shown at the bottom.
elements.sent-2411-106: This view provides users with an understanding of how the total cost is calculated, especially focusing on the join strategies.. D4.
elements.sent-2411-107: This is identical to C3.. D5.
elements.sent-2411-108: When ERX visualization is activated, the recombination process for a chosen gene is depicted through an arc diagram.
elements.sent-2411-109: Please note that the selected gene should be the offspring generated in that generation.
elements.sent-2411-110: The arc diagram is efficient when dealing with a substantial number of objects, such as the relations involved in joins within this context.
elements.sent-2411-111: Each parental gene's contribution is represented by a distinct color-coded edge, and upon hovering over each relation, the corresponding edges are highlighted, showing whether they are involved in the recombination crossover.
elements.sent-2411-112: The \systemname backend is responsible for establishing a connection between the frontend, equipped with a GUI, and PostgreSQL via an API service.
elements.sent-2411-113: As shown in Figure ~\ref{fig:arc , the query submitter transmits user queries from the user interface to the dedicated PostgreSQL database.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Within PostgreSQL, the query goes through parsing, rewriting, and optimization to determine the optimal execution plan by estimating the cost of numerous possible query plans.
elements.sent-2411-115: The executor then executes this plan, and the log data are returned to the backend.
elements.sent-2411-116: Subsequently, the backend's parser formats this data into JSON with all necessary fields and information required by the \systemname user interface for visualization.
elements.sent-2411-117: The default PostgreSQL codebase provides limited visibility into its internal states; thus, \systemname introduces a minimal patch that enriches logging with detailed internal state information.
elements.sent-2411-118: For the DP Optimizer, the patch captures and logs \code{RelOptInfo instances, which contain access paths for each relation and their joins, during the query plan enumeration phase.}
elements.sent-2411-119: In the case of GEQO, it logs each step of join sequence generation, which involves creating new sequences through recombination crossover techniques.
elements.sent-2411-120: These internal states are written to a log file during the process of PostgreSQL query optimization.
elements.sent-2411-121: Upon receiving query results from PostgreSQL, the backend processes the log file.
elements.sent-2411-122: Given that log data is plain text that is lengthy and unstructured, a parser is crucial in converting it into more readable and structured data.
elements.sent-2411-123: The parser first determines the optimizer type and adds it to the result data.
elements.sent-2411-124: For the DP Optimizer, the parser processes \code{RelOptInfo to extract essential information such as path lists, path costs, and the cheapest path, presenting it in JSON format suitable for visualization.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Similarly, for GEQO, the parser extracts generation information, including join sequences and their costs, and transforms it into JSON format.
elements.sent-2411-126: The parsed data are included in the response data together with the query result, then it is passed to the user interface for visualization.
elements.sent-2411-127: This section illustrates the user experience with \systemname through two main scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-128: The demonstration aims to showcase the interactive features of \systemname and its effectiveness in both learning and query performance optimization.
elements.sent-2411-129: Both scenarios start with users entering their custom queries or utilizing presets and then proceeding to select the database.. Dynamic Programming Optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-130: When the DAG in C1 is constructed, users observe all the scan types, join sequences, and join strategies considered during the query optimization.
elements.sent-2411-131: By clicking the play button, the interface simulates the optimization process, allowing users to explore how the optimal join sequences and cheapest access paths were determined step by step.
elements.sent-2411-132: For those curious about costs and the rationale behind the optimizer's decision, clicking on a node reveals detailed cost information in C2.. Genetic Query Optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-133: The grid heatmap representing the evolution of join sequences is constructed in D1.
elements.sent-2411-134: Users observe the convergence towards best fitness (\ie, cost), highlighting GEQO's power in improving the overall fitness of query plans over successive generations.
elements.sent-2411-135: Users select any gene within the heatmap, then a tree representing join sequences and strategies with a stacked bar chart for cost is displayed in D3.
elements.sent-2411-136: Users inspect specific generations by adjusting the range through sliders.
elements.sent-2411-137: When the selected range is sufficiently small, the color-coded edges appear, and by clicking the offspring, users trace the origin of each edge of the join sequence in D5.
elements.sent-2411-138: Moving on to D2, users monitor the cost across generations with various metrics.
elements.sent-2411-139: Database professionals may opt to optimize GEQO by using alternative recombination mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-140: They will evaluate the performance of their customized GEQO by comparing the grid heatmap, cost chart, and optimal query plan in D4.
elements.sent-2411-141: In both scenarios, users who wish to explore unselected access paths input hints in the editor using the \code{pg\_hint\_plan extension.}
elements.sent-2411-142: This will demonstrate how tweaking the query plan affects the optimization process, optimal query plan, and total cost.
elements.sent-2411-143: We propose \systemname, a visualization tool designed for PostgreSQL's query optimizers.
elements.sent-2411-144: Our demonstration shows that \systemname benefits learners and professionals with insights to enhance query performance or optimizer effectively by elucidating the complex decision-making process of optimizers.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Query optimizers are essential components of relational database management systems that directly impact query performance as they transform input queries into efficient execution plans.
elements.sent-2411-146: While users can obtain the final execution plan using the \code{EXPLAIN command and leverage existing visualization tools for intuitive understanding, the internal decision-making processes of query optimizers are hidden from users, making it difficult to understand how the plan is constructed.}
elements.sent-2411-147: To address this challenge, we present \systemnamens, an interactive visualization tool designed to explore the query optimization process in PostgreSQL.
elements.sent-2411-148: It also includes features that allow users to participate in optimization by providing hints, tuning parameters, and reusing prior optimization results.
elements.sent-2411-149: The source code, data, and/or other artifacts have been made available at \url{https://github.com/orgs/snu-jovis .}
elements.sent-2411-150: Query optimizers are a core component of relational database management systems (RDBMS) as they play a key role in determining query performance.
elements.sent-2411-151: They transform declarative SQL queries into efficient execution plans by exploring a vast search space and selecting the most optimal plan.
elements.sent-2411-152: At the same time, query optimization is one of the most challenging aspects of DBMS design, primarily due to the NP-hard nature of finding an optimal join order~\cite{np-hard and the complexity of picking the best physical operators for each logical operation.}
elements.sent-2411-153: These challenges have driven decades of research, from the traditional cost-based optimizers such as System R~\cite{systemR to recent machine learning-based approaches~\cite{bao, balsa .}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Understanding the decision-making process of the query optimizer greatly benefits both learners and practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-155: For learners, it provides a grasp of query optimization beyond the high-level descriptions of execution plans, such as how join orders and physical operators are determined.
elements.sent-2411-156: For practitioners, deeper insight into how plans are generated, including which alternatives were considered, how the costs were estimated and compared, and the rationale behind the final plan, helps explain suboptimal decisions and enables manual tuning using techniques like hints or configuration adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-157: Furthermore, such transparency is essential for extending or improving the optimizer as it clarifies its underlying strategies and implementation.
elements.sent-2411-158: Despite its importance, the query optimization process remains largely opaque to users unless they delve into the source code.
elements.sent-2411-159: While DBMSs like PostgreSQL~\cite{postgresql and MySQL~\cite{mysql provide \code{EXPLAIN and \code{ANALYZE commands to display the final execution plan and its estimated cost, along with external tools PEV2~\cite{pev2 and MySQL Workbench~\cite{workbench that offer graphical representations of the plan, these outputs only reflect the outcome of the optimization process rather than the reasoning behind it.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: Several research efforts have aimed to make query optimization more accessible through visualization and interactive features.
elements.sent-2411-161: QO-Insight~\cite{qo-insight focuses on visualizing query execution traces of steered query optimizers, which use hints to correct planning mistakes and MOCHA~\cite{mocha enables users to analyze the impact of alternative physical operator choices.}}
elements.sent-2411-162: While these works visualize and help compare the query execution plans, they fall short of providing a comprehensive view of the internal decision-making process of query optimizers.
elements.sent-2411-163: In this demonstration, we present \systemnamens, an interactive visualization tool designed to reveal the query optimization process of PostgreSQL.
elements.sent-2411-164: Unlike existing tools that focus solely on the final execution plan, \systemname provides visibility into how the optimizer constructs the plan.
elements.sent-2411-165: It visualizes the explored search space, the evaluated join orders and physical operators, and the cost estimations that drive plan selection.
elements.sent-2411-166: Beyond visualization, \systemname introduces user-guided optimization, allowing users to steer the optimizer toward better decisions or explore untapped search spaces.
elements.sent-2411-167: By making the optimizer transparent and interactive, \systemname not only bridges the gap between high-level execution plans and the underlying optimization logic but also helps users diagnose suboptimal choices and improve query performance.
elements.sent-2411-169: Comprehensive visualizations of PostgreSQL's optimization process, covering both its cost-based optimizer using dynamic programming and genetic algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-170: An in-depth view of the cost model, including cost estimation formulas that illustrate how costs impact plan selection.
elements.sent-2411-171: User-guided optimization, an interactive feature that allows users to guide the optimizer through hints, configuration adjustments, and leveraging prior optimization results.
elements.sent-2411-172: We chose PostgreSQL as our starting point due to its wide adoption and two complementary optimization approaches.
elements.sent-2411-173: Our tool is designed to be extensible, with future work planned to support additional DBMSs.
elements.sent-2411-174: Picasso~\cite{picasso provides graphical profiling and analysis of query optimizer behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-175: QE3D~\cite{qe3d introduces a three-dimensional representation of distributed query plans, helping users identify key performance aspects.}
elements.sent-2411-176: DBinsight~\cite{dbinsight visualizes the query processing pipeline and unifies heterogeneous data structures across RDBMSs through a common interface.}
elements.sent-2411-177: MOCHA~\cite{mocha explores the impact of physical operator selection by allowing users to compare alternative plans and observe corresponding performance changes.}
elements.sent-2411-178: QO-Insight~\cite{qo-insight targets steered query optimizers that use hints to address planning mistakes, offering a visual inspection of query execution traces.}
elements.sent-2411-179: A variety of visualization tools for query optimizers have been developed.
elements.sent-2411-180: Postgres Explain Visualizer (PEV)~\cite{pev and its successor PEV2~\cite{pev2 render \code{EXPLAIN output into interactive trees, enabling users to analyze the structure and estimated costs of execution plans.}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Similarly, MySQL Workbench~\cite{workbench provides graphical plan visualization for MySQL as part of a broader suite of database modeling and administration tools.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Efforts have also been made to visualize genetic algorithm~\cite{cruz , which inspired \systemnamens's approach to GEQO visualization.}
elements.sent-2411-183: However, our system is tailored specifically to GEQO, leveraging unique data extracted from optimizer logs and offering interactive features for genetic query optimization.
elements.sent-2411-184: To the best of our knowledge, \systemname is the first tool to provide dedicated visualization for GEQO.
elements.sent-2411-185: The standard optimizer builds these paths by enumerating possible plans using dynamic programming~\cite{dp and evaluates them with a cost model to determine the cheapest path.}
elements.sent-2411-186: However, this approach becomes infeasible for queries involving a large number of joins, as exploring the exponentially growing search space requires excessive time and memory.
elements.sent-2411-187: In such cases, PostgreSQL switches to the Genetic Query Optimizer (GEQO), which applies a genetic algorithm to iteratively generate and refine join sequences over successive generations using recombination mechanisms, balancing optimization quality with computational efficiency~\cite{pg-doc-geqo .}

elements.sent-2411-188: This section provides an overview of the architecture of \systemnamens, illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:arc .}
elements.sent-2411-189: Users submit queries through the frontend's SQL editor, which are transmitted to PostgreSQL via the backend.
elements.sent-2411-190: PostgreSQL processes the queries and generates detailed optimizer logs, which are collected and parsed by the backend into structured JavaScript Object Notation (JSON).
elements.sent-2411-191: The processed data is then sent to the frontend, where it is visualized through an interactive interface.
elements.sent-2411-192: We applied a lightweight patch to PostgreSQL 16.2 to add detailed logging of the optimizer’s internal decisions, including paths and costs.
elements.sent-2411-193: Since the patch is non-intrusive, the system can be easily extended to other PostgreSQL versions.
elements.sent-2411-194: Figure~\ref{fig:full represents the graphical user interface (GUI) designed to offer an intuitive and interactive experience for users to explore and understand the query optimization process.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Built with React and D3.js~\cite{d3 , the interface leverages data-driven visualizations to present complex optimization details in an accessible manner.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Sidebar \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {A provides a flexible interface for managing query planning visualizations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: It consists of three components: (\rom{1 ) Queries allows users to select, deselect, or delete submitted queries, supporting side-by-side comparison of multiple queries and their optimizations.}
elements.sent-2411-198: This helps users analyze how different queries or configurations affect the optimizer's decisions, (\rom{2 ) History maintains a record of previously executed queries, enabling quick reuse and reducing repetitive input and (\rom{3 ) Presets includes predefined queries from widely used benchmarks, TPC-H~\cite{tpch , TPC-DS~\cite{tpcds , and Join Order Benchmark (JOB)~\cite{job .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-199: It simplifies experimentation and performance evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-200: SQL editor \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {B allows users to submit queries, select a database, and execute them for analysis.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: For both the query planning views \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {C and \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D , users can switch to the \code{EXPLAIN tab to visualize the \code{EXPLAIN output as a hierarchical tree as presented in Figure~\ref{fig:explain .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: This view provides an intuitive understanding of the final execution plan.
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{tikzpicture} \node[inner sep=0pt, rounded corners=5pt, clip] {\includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/subquery.png}}; \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Subquery Support in TPC-H Query 11} \label{fig:subquery}
elements.sent-2411-203: Query Planning View \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {C visualizes PostgreSQL's standard optimization process using dynamic programming.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: The optimizer adopts a bottom-up approach, starting by determining access paths for each base relation.
elements.sent-2411-205: It then incrementally constructs join sequences and selects physical operators until the complete execution plan is achieved.
elements.sent-2411-206: To effectively depict the process, \systemname employs a directed acyclic graph (DAG), which is well-suited for representing the bottom-up strategy due to its ability to handle multiple parent nodes and hierarchical relationships.
elements.sent-2411-207: In this structure, circular nodes represent relations or join sequences, while rectangular nodes represent physical operators considered during plan enumeration.
elements.sent-2411-208: Edges indicate the application of an operator to its input relations or join orders.
elements.sent-2411-209: The DAG is presented in an inverted layout where operators appear as children of their associated relations.
elements.sent-2411-210: For example, the left DAG in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {C1 shows multiple scan methods evaluated for each relation, and nested loop join and hash join were explored for joining two relations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-211: Furthermore, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:subquery for TPC-H Query 11, \systemname supports subqueries by generating separate DAGs for each one, making it possible to inspect complex queries in a modular way.}
elements.sent-2411-212: Users can interact with the DAG through several features.
elements.sent-2411-213: The Play/Stop button in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {C1 animates the optimization process, reducing the opacity of non-selected nodes to make the decision path easy to track.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: The Cost Scale toggle adjusts the size of operator nodes based on their estimated cost, highlighting why specific operators or join sequences were chosen.
elements.sent-2411-215: The right DAG shows the result after activating both features.
elements.sent-2411-216: Additionally, clicking on an operator node reveals detailed cost factors and calculation formulas in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {C2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: The query needs to access 74,900 tuples out of a total of 6,001,215 ($N_{rows}$).
elements.sent-2411-218: Although the bitmap heap scan incurs a startup cost for building the list of disk pages to fetch, it significantly reduces the number of pages ($N_{pages}$) and tuples ($N_{tuples}$) accessed.
elements.sent-2411-219: This reduction in disk I/O and CPU costs for scanning only the relevant records makes the bitmap heap scan more efficient than a sequential scan, which fetches all pages and processes every record.
elements.sent-2411-220: Due to the query's low selectivity, the bitmap heap scan achieves a much lower cost, leading the optimizer to select it.
elements.sent-2411-221: As demonstrated in this example, users can understand the rationale behind the optimizer's decision by examining the cost.
elements.sent-2411-222: By combining these features, \systemname helps users visually grasp all paths considered during optimization and the rationale behind the optimizer's decisions, including detailed cost calculations.
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{subfigure}{0.5\columnwidth} \begin{tikzpicture} \node[inner sep=0pt, rounded corners=5pt, clip] {\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/geqo-range.png}}; \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Range Sliders} \label{fig:geqo-range} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.4\columnwidth} \begin{tikzpicture} \node[inner sep=0pt, rounded corners=5pt, clip] {\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/geqo-stack.png}}; \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Stacked Bar Chart} \label{fig:geqo-stack} \end{subfigure} \caption{Interactive Features for GEQO} \label{fig:demo-geqo}
elements.sent-2411-223: Query Planning View \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D visualizes PostgreSQL's GEQO, which applies a genetic algorithm to heuristically explore join orders when exhaustive enumeration becomes impractical (\ie, when the number of relations involved in joins reaches or exceeds the PostgreSQL's configurable parameter \code{geqo\_threshold ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: GEQO iteratively refines a population of candidate join sequences, referred to as genes, through selection, crossover, and replacement.
elements.sent-2411-225: Each generation maintains a pool of genes, where lower-cost genes are considered more fit.
elements.sent-2411-226: New genes are generated via crossover, combining segments of two parent genes to produce offspring.
elements.sent-2411-227: The pool is then sorted by estimated cost and the least fit genes are discarded.
elements.sent-2411-228: This process continues until a preset number of join sequences are evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-229: The heatmap \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D1 represents the pool of genes across generations, with color gradients to indicate their estimated costs.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-230: This effectively demonstrates how GEQO improves fitness over time.
elements.sent-2411-231: In addition, a line chart \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D2 tracks the best, worst, median, and average costs throughout the optimization process, providing an overview of cost trends.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-232: Users can interact with the visualization to explore the optimization process in detail.
elements.sent-2411-233: The range sliders in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D1 allow users to focus on specific generations or genes.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:geqo-range , when zoomed in on a smaller range, crossover visualizations appear, illustrating which parent genes contribute to offspring through edges.}
elements.sent-2411-235: In the cost chart \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D2 , users can hover over lines to pinpoint the specific generation, toggle between linear and logarithmic scales, and click on legends to isolate metrics for focused analysis.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: Clicking on a gene in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D1 displays its join sequence as a tree in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D3 , with operator costs represented as a stacked bar chart.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-237: Users can click on each stack to see how much the corresponding operator accounts for the total cost.
elements.sent-2411-238: As presented in Figure~\ref{fig:geqo-stack , selecting a stack highlights the related operator node in red and displays its cost and percentage at the bottom.}
elements.sent-2411-239: When the selected gene is newly generated in the current generation, the crossover visualization in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D4 is activated.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-240: An arc diagram shows how parent genes' join sequences contribute to the offspring's join sequence using color-coded edges.
elements.sent-2411-241: Moreover, users can rerun the same query with prior optimization results and customized configuration in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D5 (See Section~\ref{sec:guided-geqo for details).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: These dedicated visualizations and interactive features make the complex and opaque GEQO process accessible and understandable.
elements.sent-2411-243: Users can observe the optimizer's evolutionary behavior, understand why certain join sequences are favored, analyze cost distributions, and explore how crossover operations are executed.
elements.sent-2411-244: The \systemname backend connects the frontend GUI with PostgreSQL through an Application Protocol Interface service.
elements.sent-2411-245: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:arc , the query submitter transmits user queries to PostgreSQL, where they undergo parsing, rewriting, and optimization to determine the optimal execution plan.}
elements.sent-2411-246: The executor then runs the execution plan, and relevant log data are returned to the backend.
elements.sent-2411-247: The backend parser processes this data and formats it into JSON, including all necessary fields for visualization in the user interface.
elements.sent-2411-248: To enhance transparency into the optimization process, \systemname introduces a minimal patch to PostgreSQL that enriches its logs with detailed optimizer state data.
elements.sent-2411-249: For the standard optimizer, the patch captures information generated during query plan enumeration, including access paths, path costs, and join sequences.
elements.sent-2411-250: In the case of GEQO, it records each step of join sequence generation and crossover operations.
elements.sent-2411-251: These internal states are written to a log file during query optimization.
elements.sent-2411-252: Since the log contains unstructured plain text, the parser is crucial for extracting and converting the data into a structured, readable format.
elements.sent-2411-253: It first identifies the optimizer type and then extracts the corresponding data: path lists, costs, and the cheapest paths for the standard optimizer; generation information, join sequences, and associated costs for GEQO.
elements.sent-2411-254: The parsed data are formatted into JSON, combined with the query result, and passed to the frontend for visualization.
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{subfigure}{0.9\columnwidth} \begin{lstlisting}[language=SQL] /*+ SeqScan(sales_returns) NestLoop(sales_returns catalog_page date_dim) */ \end{lstlisting} \caption{Hint} \label{fig:hint} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.5\columnwidth} \begin{tikzpicture} \node[inner sep=0pt, rounded corners=5pt, clip] {\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/ageqo.png}}; \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Guided GEQO} \label{fig:ageqo} \end{subfigure} \caption{User-Guided Optimization} \label{fig:user-guided}
elements.sent-2411-255: By influencing the optimizer's decisions or reusing previous optimization results, users can diagnose suboptimal plans, explore alternative plans, and improve query performance in a controlled and intuitive manner.
elements.sent-2411-256: This capability is useful in scenarios where the optimizer selects suboptimal plans or when manual tuning is required.
elements.sent-2411-257: By providing hints, users can override the optimizer's decisions and visually analyze the impact of hints on the optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-258: Since PostgreSQL does not natively support hints, \systemname integrates the \code{pg\_hint\_plan extension~\cite{pg-hint by applying a minor patch to add logging capabilities to the extension.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: Hints are specified using a special comment syntax, prefixed with \code{/*+ and ending with \code{*/ , as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:hint .}}}
elements.sent-2411-260: In this example, users apply a hint to TPC-H Query 14 to enforce a nested loop join (right DAG), overriding the optimizer's choice of a hash join (left DAG).
elements.sent-2411-261: This change in the join method also impacts the access paths for base relations.
elements.sent-2411-262: In the hash join plan, the optimizer sequentially scans the \code{part relation to build a hash table and then probes it with the \code{lineitem relation.}}
elements.sent-2411-263: In contrast, the nested loop join plan iterates over each record in the \code{lineitem relation and looks up matching rows in the \code{part relation.}}
elements.sent-2411-264: The join condition is \code{l\_partkey = p\_partkey , where \code{p\_partkey is the primary key of the \code{part .}}}
elements.sent-2411-265: PostgreSQL automatically creates an index on primary key columns, allowing the optimizer to perform an index scan on \code{p\_partkey .}
elements.sent-2411-266: As a result, for each row in \code{lineitem , matching records can be efficiently retrieved from \code{part using the index.}}
elements.sent-2411-267: As such, users can experiment with different execution plans and observe their impact on the optimization process.
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{subfigure}{0.45\columnwidth} \begin{tikzpicture} \node[inner sep=0pt, rounded corners=5pt, clip] {\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/ageqo-before.png}}; \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Default Join Order} \label{fig:ageqo-before} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\columnwidth} \begin{tikzpicture} \node[inner sep=0pt, rounded corners=5pt, clip] {\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/ageqo-after.png}}; \end{tikzpicture} \caption{User-Guided Join Order} \label{fig:ageqo-after} \end{subfigure} \caption{Guided GEQO in JOB Query 10c} \label{fig:demo-geqo}
elements.sent-2411-268: This feature is especially useful for analytical workloads, where the same queries are often executed repeatedly.
elements.sent-2411-269: By leveraging prior optimization results and tuning configurations, users can improve the optimality of the execution plan and explore the larger search space.
elements.sent-2411-270: From the first two input fields in Figure~\ref{fig:ageqo , users can customize the initialization of the pool.}
elements.sent-2411-271: They can specify the number of high-performing genes to include (\eg, the top 5 join sequences with the minimum cost from a prior run) or provide user-defined join sequences.
elements.sent-2411-272: Above the second input field, each relation's unique ID and name are given.
elements.sent-2411-273: Users can define join orders by entering space-delimited gene IDs and specify multiple join orders by concatenating them with commas.
elements.sent-2411-274: This guided initialization ensures that the genetic algorithm begins with a combination of proven solutions and user-defined candidates, increasing the likelihood of discovering better execution plans while avoiding the inefficiencies of random initialization.
elements.sent-2411-275: From the last two input fields in Figure~\ref{fig:ageqo , users can adjust the \code{geqo\_seed , which controls the random initialization of the pool to ensure reproducibility, and the \code{selection\_bias , which determines the selective pressure applied during parent selection in crossover.}}}
elements.sent-2411-276: By tuning these parameters, users can balance exploration (\ie, maintaining diversity among genes) and exploitation (\ie, intensifying the search around high-fitness genes).
elements.sent-2411-277: To show the effectiveness of user-guided optimization in GEQO, we use JOB Query 10c as an example.
elements.sent-2411-278: Figure~\ref{fig:ageqo-before shows the query plan chosen by GEQO and its associated cost in the default configuration, with \code{geqo\_seed set to 0 and \code{selection\_bias set to 2.}}}
elements.sent-2411-279: By rerunning the query using \systemname's user-guided optimization feature as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:ageqo , users set a different \code{geqo\_seed value, focus on diversity by lowering \code{selection\_bias to 1.5, and initialize the population with the fiver lowest-cost genes from the previous run.}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: As a result, GEQO discovers a more efficient execution plan, reducing the cost by 7\%, as seen in Figure~\ref{fig:ageqo-after .}
elements.sent-2411-281: This shows \systemnamens's ability to improve query performance through guided exploration of join sequences in GEQO.
elements.sent-2411-282: In the demonstration, we will showcase the user experience with \systemname through two representative scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-283: The demonstration highlights the effectiveness of \systemname in both educational and practical contexts, helping users understand and participate in query optimization.
elements.sent-2411-284: In each scenario, users begin by selecting a query from the Presets or History panel in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {A or submitting a custom query through \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {B and then choose the target database.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-285: Once the query is processed, users can explore tailored optimization visualizations in the Query Planning tab and view the final execution plan by switching to the \code{EXPLAIN tab.}
elements.sent-2411-286: In the \code{EXPLAIN tab, users can zoom in and out, reposition the tree, and collapse or expand nodes by clicking on them.}
elements.sent-2411-287: This interaction is to simplify complex plans by focusing on specific parts of the execution plan for closer inspection.
elements.sent-2411-288: Users can customize the query view layout and perform side-by-side analysis of multiple query plans using the Queries panel in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {A .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-289: By toggling the checkboxes, users can selectively show or hide views for specific queries, and by clicking the trash icon, they can permanently remove a query view.
elements.sent-2411-290: In scenarios where dynamic programming is applied for query optimization, \systemname constructs a DAG in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {C1 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-291: The DAG presents the scan methods for base relations, join orders, and join strategies considered during query planning.
elements.sent-2411-292: Users can zoom in and out and reposition the DAG to focus on specific portions of the planning.
elements.sent-2411-293: By clicking the Play button, users can simulate the step-by-step construction of the final execution plan and observe how the optimizer selects the cheapest paths based on estimated costs.
elements.sent-2411-294: Toggling the Show Cost Scale adjusts the size of operator nodes according to their estimated cost, helping users identify cost-intensive operations at a glance.
elements.sent-2411-295: For deeper analysis, users can click on a node to examine detailed cost information in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {C2 , including the cost factors, calculation formulas, and the values used in the calculation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-296: Selected nodes are highlighted with a red outline and can be selected or deselected individually.
elements.sent-2411-297: Users can select multiple nodes to compare cost calculations across operators.
elements.sent-2411-298: For user-guided optimization using hints, users can add directives in the SQL editor \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {B .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-299: Hints are written at the top of the query, enclosed within \code{/*+ and \code{*/ , and consist of physical operators and relations in parentheses, separated by whitespaces.}}
elements.sent-2411-300: After submitting the query with hints, users can observe how the optimizer incorporates their guidance into the plan.
elements.sent-2411-301: In scenarios where GEQO is applied, \systemname provides an interactive heatmap in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D1 to visualize the evolution of join sequences, represented as genes, over generations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-302: Users can observe how GEQO explores the search space and progressively converges toward plans with better fitness, reflected by lower estimated costs.
elements.sent-2411-303: The Cost Chart \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D2 tracks overall cost trends across generations using various metrics.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-304: Users can hover over lines to view precise cost values at specific generations and selectively display metrics by clicking the legend.
elements.sent-2411-305: For deeper exploration, users can hover over a gene within the heatmap to view a tooltip displaying its generation, fitness rank, and estimated cost.
elements.sent-2411-306: Upon selection, \systemname presents a detailed view in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D3 that includes the join order and physical operators in a tree, along with a stacked bar chart that breaks down the estimated costs by the operator.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-307: Users can further focus on specific generations or subsets of genes by adjusting range sliders in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D1 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-308: When the selected range is sufficiently small, \systemname visualizes the parent-child relationships produced by crossover using edges.
elements.sent-2411-309: By selecting an offspring gene, crossover visualization \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D4 traces the origin of each edge in its join sequence, revealing how sequences are inherited or mutated.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-310: If the edge is inherited from both parents, it is displayed as a split color, while mutated edges are shown in gray.
elements.sent-2411-311: For guided GEQO, users can rerun the query by specifying the top $N$ genes to initialize the pool, defining join orders if desired, and adjusting \code{geqo\_seed and \code{selection\_bias parameters directly in \colorbox{black {\textcolor{white {D5 .. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}}}}}}}
